infobox.value: Motorola Mobility Holdings, Inc. (4 January 2011 –17 October 2014)[[1]](#cite_note-1)

(stylized in all-lowercase as **motorola**), is a Chinese-owned, United States-based [consumer electronics](/wiki/Consumer_electronics "Consumer electronics") manufacturer primarily producing [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") and other mobile devices running [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)"). Motorola is headquartered at [Merchandise Mart](/wiki/Merchandise_Mart "Merchandise Mart") in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), Illinois, and has been a subsidiary of [Lenovo](/wiki/Lenovo "Lenovo") since 2014.[[4]](#cite_note-Chinese_company_Lenovo_bought_Motorola-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American consumer electronics company

This article is about the electronics company. For the safety and security company, see [Motorola Solutions](/wiki/Motorola_Solutions "Motorola Solutions"). For the predecessor company, see [Motorola](/wiki/Motorola "Motorola").

"Moto (smartphone)" redirects here. For the product line of this name, see [Motorola Moto](/wiki/Motorola_Moto "Motorola Moto").

**Motorola Mobility LLC**, marketed as **Motorola** (stylized in all-lowercase as **motorola**), is a Chinese-owned, United States-based [consumer electronics](/wiki/Consumer_electronics "Consumer electronics") manufacturer primarily producing [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") and other mobile devices running [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)"). Motorola is headquartered at [Merchandise Mart](/wiki/Merchandise_Mart "Merchandise Mart") in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), Illinois, and has been a subsidiary of [Lenovo](/wiki/Lenovo "Lenovo") since 2014.[[4]](#cite_note-Chinese_company_Lenovo_bought_Motorola-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Motorola Mobility was formed on January 4, 2011, after a split of the original [Motorola Inc.](/wiki/Motorola_Inc. "Motorola Inc.") into two separate companies, with Motorola Mobility assuming the company's consumer-oriented product lines, including its [mobile phone](/wiki/Mobile_phone "Mobile phone") business, as well as its [cable modems](/wiki/Cable_modem "Cable modem") and pay television [set-top boxes](/wiki/Set-top_box "Set-top box"), while the enterprise and radio communication division continues as [Motorola Solutions](/wiki/Motorola_Solutions "Motorola Solutions"), a legal successor of the original company. In May 2012, [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") acquired Motorola Mobility for US$12.5 billion; the main intent of the purchase was to gain Motorola Mobility's [patent](/wiki/Patent "Patent") portfolio, in order to protect other Android vendors from litigation.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Shortly after the purchase, Google sold Motorola Mobility's cable modem and set-top box business to [Arris Group](/wiki/Arris_Group "Arris Group"), and products increasingly focused on entry-level smartphones. Under the [ATAP](/wiki/Google_ATAP "Google ATAP") division, Google also began development on [Project Ara](/wiki/Project_Ara "Project Ara"). In October 2014, Google sold Motorola Mobility for $2.91 billion to Lenovo, which excluded ATAP and most of the patents.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Lenovo's [existing smartphone division](/wiki/Lenovo_smartphones "Lenovo smartphones") was subsumed by Motorola Mobility.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The company currently sells a range of smartphones, mainly consisting of the high-end [Edge](/wiki/Motorola_Edge "Motorola Edge") series, the [Razr](/wiki/Motorola_Razr "Motorola Razr") series of foldables, the [Moto](/wiki/Motorola_Moto "Motorola Moto") G series, as well as a number of other series and products depending on region. As of 2025, its current flagship device is the Motorola Razr 60 Ultra.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [Motorola](/wiki/Motorola "Motorola")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Motorola_Mobility.svg/250px-Motorola_Mobility.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Motorola_Mobility.svg)

The company's original logo, used from 2011 to 2012

On January 4, 2011, [Motorola, Inc.](/wiki/Motorola "Motorola") was split into two publicly traded companies; [Motorola Solutions](/wiki/Motorola_Solutions "Motorola Solutions") took on the company's enterprise-oriented business units, while the remaining mostly consumer divisions were taken on by Motorola Mobility.[[12]](#cite_note-mt-12) The previous Motorola had been a pioneer in the development of [cellular phones](/wiki/Cellular_phones "Cellular phones"). After the major success of [RAZR V3](/wiki/Motorola_RAZR_V3 "Motorola RAZR V3"), the company was unable to keep up and quickly lost market share to rivals such as [Nokia](/wiki/Nokia "Nokia") and [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung_Mobile "Samsung Mobile"), leading to major financial losses that culminated in the company's split.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Legally, the split was structured so that Motorola Inc. changed its name to Motorola Solutions and spun off Motorola Mobility as a new publicly traded company.

### 2011–2012: Independent

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 2011–2012: Independent")]

Motorola Mobility originally consisted of the mobile devices business, which produced [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone"), mobile accessories including Bluetooth headphones, and the home business, which produced [set-top boxes](/wiki/Set-top_box "Set-top box"), end-to-end video solutions, cordless phones, and [cable modems](/wiki/Cable_modem "Cable modem").[[12]](#cite_note-mt-12)

In the run up to the company's formation, [Motorola Defy](/wiki/Motorola_Defy "Motorola Defy"), [Droid Pro](/wiki/Droid_Pro "Droid Pro"), [Droid 2](/wiki/Droid_2 "Droid 2") (global) and Bravo were among the handsets introduced. At the 2011 [Consumer Electronic Show](/wiki/Consumer_Electronic_Show "Consumer Electronic Show"), Motorola Mobility introduced the [Motorola Atrix 4G](/wiki/Motorola_Atrix_4G "Motorola Atrix 4G"), [Droid Bionic](/wiki/Droid_Bionic "Droid Bionic") and Cliq 2 handsets, as well as the [Motorola Xoom](/wiki/Motorola_Xoom "Motorola Xoom") tablet.[[14]](#cite_note-14) At Mobile World Congress the company also announced the Motorola Gleam flip phone and [Motorola Pro](/wiki/Motorola_Pro "Motorola Pro") for GSM networks.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) In Q1 2011, the company reported revenues of $3 billion (up 22% from a year prior) and sales of 9.3 million mobile devices, including 4.1 million smartphones.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In June 2011, the company launched their third generation Droid, [Droid 3](/wiki/Droid_3 "Droid 3").[[18]](#cite_note-18) They also announced other products during this time including the [Motorola Photon 4G](/wiki/Motorola_Photon "Motorola Photon") and the Motorola Wilder [feature phone](/wiki/Feature_phone "Feature phone").[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

On October 18, 2011, Motorola Mobility announced the [Droid Razr](/wiki/Droid_Razr "Droid Razr") smartphone, bringing back the [Razr](/wiki/Motorola_Razr "Motorola Razr") brand that had been very popular as the [Razr V3](/wiki/Razr_V3 "Razr V3") in the 2000s.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### 2012–2014: Google ownership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2012–2014: Google ownership")]

On August 15, 2011, American technology company [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") announced that it would acquire Motorola Mobility for $12.5 billion, pending regulatory approval.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-ftgoogle-24) Critics viewed Google as being a [white knight](/wiki/White_knight_(business) "White knight (business)"), since Motorola Mobility had recently had a fifth straight quarter of losses.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Google planned to operate Motorola Mobility as an independent company.[[26]](#cite_note-26) In a post on the company's blog, Google CEO and co-founder [Larry Page](/wiki/Larry_Page "Larry Page") revealed that Google's acquisition of Motorola Mobility was a strategic move to strengthen Google's [patent](/wiki/Patent "Patent") portfolio. At the time, the company had 17,000 patents, with 7,500 patents pending.[[27]](#cite_note-ogblog-27)[[28]](#cite_note-cnet081511-28) The expanded portfolio was to defend the viability of its [Android](/wiki/Android_OS "Android OS") operating system, which had been the subject of numerous [patent infringement](/wiki/Patent_infringement "Patent infringement") lawsuits between device vendors and other companies such as [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc."), [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") and [Oracle](/wiki/Oracle_Corporation "Oracle Corporation").[[27]](#cite_note-ogblog-27)[[29]](#cite_note-Google_CEO-29)[[30]](#cite_note-David_Drummond,_Senior_Vice_President_and_Chief_Legal_Officer-30)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Motorola_Mobility_Logo_Being_Installed.jpg/250px-Motorola_Mobility_Logo_Being_Installed.jpg)](/wiki/File:Motorola_Mobility_Logo_Being_Installed.jpg)

The installation of the 2013–2014 Motorola Mobility logo near the main Google campus, following Google's purchase. The office on the photo has since been closed.

On November 17, 2011, Motorola Mobility announced that its shareholders voted in favor of the company's acquisition by Google for $12.5 billion. The deal received regulatory approval from the [United States Department of Justice](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") and the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") on February 13, 2012.[[31]](#cite_note-Terrence-31) The deal received subsequent approval from Chinese authorities and was completed on May 22, 2012.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Alongside the completion of the acquisition, Motorola Mobility's CEO [Sanjay Jha](/wiki/Sanjay_Jha_(businessman) "Sanjay Jha (businessman)") was replaced by [Dennis Woodside](/wiki/Dennis_Woodside "Dennis Woodside"), a former senior vice president at Google.[[33]](#cite_note-dorazio2012verge-33)

On August 13, 2012, Google announced that it would cut 4,000 employees and close one third of the company's locations, mostly outside the US.[[34]](#cite_note-ABC081312-34)

On December 19, 2012, it was announced that [Arris Group](/wiki/Arris_Group "Arris Group") would purchase Motorola Mobility's [cable modem](/wiki/Cable_modem "Cable modem") and [set-top box](/wiki/Set-top_box "Set-top box") business for $2.35 billion in a cash-and-stock transaction.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

In May 2013, Motorola Mobility opened a factory in [Fort Worth](/wiki/Fort_Worth "Fort Worth"), [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"), with the intent to assemble customized smartphones in the US. At its peak, the factory employed 3,800 workers.[[36]](#cite_note-wsj-fwplantclosing-36) On April 9, 2014, following the departure of Woodside, lead product developer [Rick Osterloh](/wiki/Rick_Osterloh "Rick Osterloh") was named the new president of Motorola Mobility.[[37]](#cite_note-engadget-newceo-37)

Under Google ownership, Motorola Mobility's market share would be boosted by a focus on high-quality entry-level smartphones, aimed primarily at [emerging markets](/wiki/Emerging_market "Emerging market"); in the first quarter of 2014, Motorola Mobility sold 6.5 million phones—led by strong sales of its low-end [Moto G](/wiki/Moto_G_(1st_generation) "Moto G (1st generation)"), especially in markets such as India, and in the United Kingdom—where the company accounted for 6% of smartphone sales sold in the quarter, up from nearly zero. These goals were compounded further by the May 2014 introduction of the [Moto E](/wiki/Moto_E_(1st_generation) "Moto E (1st generation)")—a low-end device aimed at first-time smartphone owners in emerging markets.[[38]](#cite_note-cnet-uksales-38)[[39]](#cite_note-cnet-motogsales-39)[[40]](#cite_note-guardian-motoeindia-40)[[41]](#cite_note-wsj-motoeindia-41) In May 2014, Motorola Mobility announced that it would close its Fort Worth factory by the end of the year, citing the high costs of domestic manufacturing in combination with the weak sales of the [Moto X](/wiki/Moto_X_(1st_generation) "Moto X (1st generation)") (which was customized and assembled at the plant) and the company's increased emphasis on low-end devices and emerging markets.[[36]](#cite_note-wsj-fwplantclosing-36)

### 2014–2019: Lenovo ownership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2014–2019: Lenovo ownership")]

On January 29, 2014, Google announced it would, pending regulatory approval, sell Motorola Mobility to the Hong Kong based Chinese technology company [Lenovo](/wiki/Lenovo "Lenovo") for US$2.91 billion in a cash-and-stock deal,[[4]](#cite_note-Chinese_company_Lenovo_bought_Motorola-4) seeing the sale of $750 million in Lenovo shares to Google. Google retained the Advanced Technologies & Projects unit (which was integrated into the main Android team), and all but 2000 of the company's patents. Lenovo had prominently disclosed its intent to enter the U.S. smartphone market, and had previously expressed interest in acquiring [BlackBerry](/wiki/BlackBerry_Limited "BlackBerry Limited"), but was reportedly blocked by the Canadian government due to national security concerns.[[42]](#cite_note-verge-lenovosale-42)[[43]](#cite_note-verge-bblenovo-43)[[44]](#cite_note-verge-aragoogle-44)[[45]](#cite_note-ndtv-stake-45) The acquisition was completed on October 30, 2014. The company remained headquartered in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), and continued to use the Motorola brand, while Liu Jun (刘军) — president of Lenovo's mobile device business, became the company's chairman.[[46]](#cite_note-wsj-lenovoclosed-46)

On January 26, 2015, owing to its new ownership, Motorola Mobility re-launched its product line in China with the local release of the [second-generation Moto X](/wiki/Moto_X_(2nd_generation) "Moto X (2nd generation)"), and an upcoming release of the [Moto G LTE](/wiki/Moto_G_(1st_generation) "Moto G (1st generation)") and Moto X Pro (a re-branded [Nexus 6](/wiki/Nexus_6 "Nexus 6")) in time for the [Chinese New Year](/wiki/Chinese_New_Year "Chinese New Year").[[47]](#cite_note-verge-motochina-47)

Lenovo maintained a "hands-off" approach in regards to Motorola Mobility's product development. Head designer Jim Wicks explained that "Google had very little influence and Lenovo has been the same." The company continued to engage in practices it adopted under Google, such as the use of nearly "stock" Android, undercutting competitor's pricing while offering superior hardware (as further encouraged by Lenovo), and placing a larger focus on direct-to-consumer selling of unlocked phones in the US market (as opposed to carrier subsidized versions).[[48]](#cite_note-VergeMoto-48) On July 28, 2015, Motorola Mobility unveiled three new devices, and its first under Lenovo ownership—the [third-generation Moto G](/wiki/Moto_G_(3rd_generation) "Moto G (3rd generation)"), [Moto X Play](/wiki/Moto_X_Play "Moto X Play"), and [Moto X Style](/wiki/Moto_X_Style "Moto X Style")—in three separate events.[[48]](#cite_note-VergeMoto-48)

#### Integration with Lenovo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Integration with Lenovo")]

In August 2015, Lenovo announced that it would merge its existing smartphone division, including design, development, and manufacturing, into the Motorola Mobility unit. The announcement came in addition to a cut of 3,200 jobs across the entire company.[[49]](#cite_note-49) As a result of the change, Motorola Mobility will be responsible for the development and production of its own "Moto" product line, as well as Lenovo's own "Vibe" range.[[50]](#cite_note-motorola-nextchapter-50)

In January 2016, Lenovo announced that the "Motorola" name would be further downplayed in public usage in favor of the "Moto" brand. Motorola Mobility later clarified that the "Motorola" brand will continue to be used in product packaging and through its brand licensees. The company said that "the Motorola legacy is near and dear to us as product designers, engineers and Motorola [Mobility] employees, and clearly it's important to many of you who have had long relationships with us. We plan to continue it under our parent company, Lenovo."[[50]](#cite_note-motorola-nextchapter-50)

In response to claims by a Lenovo executive that only high-end devices would be produced under the "Moto" name, with low-end devices being amalgamated into Lenovo's existing "Vibe" brand, Motorola Mobility clarified its plans and explained that it would continue to release low-end products under the Moto brand, including the popular Moto G and Moto E lines. Motorola Mobility stated that there would be overlap between the Vibe and Moto lines in some price points and territories, but that both brands would have different "identities" and experiences. Moto devices would be positioned as "innovative" and "trendsetting" products, and Vibe would be a "mass-market challenger brand".[[51]](#cite_note-verge-vibemoto-51)[[52]](#cite_note-cnet-nomoremotorola-52)[[53]](#cite_note-mashable-notkilling-53)

In November 2016, it was reported that Lenovo would be branding all its future smartphones under the brand "Moto by Lenovo".[[54]](#cite_note-campaign-motoname-54)[[55]](#cite_note-ibt-motobrand-55) In March 2017, it was reported that Lenovo would continue to use the "Motorola" brand and logo, citing its [recognition](/wiki/Brand_recognition "Brand recognition") as a heritage cellphone brand. Furthermore, Aymar de Lencquesaing, Motorola Mobility's president at the time, stated that Lenovo planned to phase out its self-branded smartphones in favor of the Motorola brand.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

### 2020s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2020s")]

Under Lenovo, Motorola Mobility has faced criticism for having an increasingly poor commitment to maintaining Android software updates for its devices, exemplified by negative responses to a 2019 announcement that [Android 9.0 "Pie"](/wiki/Android_Pie "Android Pie") updates to the Moto Z2 Force in the United States would only be available to the [Verizon Wireless](/wiki/Verizon_Wireless "Verizon Wireless") model.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)

In the early 2020s, Motorola Mobility managed to double its global smartphone shipments (2020-2024). During this period, its market share in the U.S. has increased from 4.8% to 11.2%. Its strongest market is [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") where Motorola Mobility holds 31% market share in the mobile phone market as of 2024.[[59]](#cite_note-59) In addition, Motorola Mobility also marks its return to [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") in February 2025, 7 years after it left the Indonesian market in 2018.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

## Products

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Products")]

### Razr

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Razr")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Black-RAZRV3-closed.jpg/250px-Black-RAZRV3-closed.jpg)](/wiki/File:Black-RAZRV3-closed.jpg)

Black RAZR V3

Motorola Mobility's predecessor Motorola, Inc. released the [Razr V3](/wiki/Motorola_Razr "Motorola Razr") in the third quarter of 2004.[[61]](#cite_note-61) Because of its striking appearance and thin profile, it was initially marketed as an exclusive fashion phone,[[62]](#cite_note-62) but within a year, its price was lowered and it was wildly successful, selling over 50 million units by July 2006.[[63]](#cite_note-63) Over the Razr four-year run, Motorola sold more than 130 million units, becoming the bestselling [clamshell](/wiki/Flip_(form) "Flip (form)") phone in the world.

Motorola released other phones based on the Razr design as part of the [4LTR](/wiki/4LTR "4LTR") line. These include the [Pebl U6](/wiki/Motorola_PEBL_U6 "Motorola PEBL U6"), [Slvr L6](/wiki/Motorola_SLVR_L6 "Motorola SLVR L6"), [Slvr L7](/wiki/Motorola_SLVR_L7 "Motorola SLVR L7") (more expensive variant of Slvr L6), [Razr V3c](/wiki/Motorola_RAZR_V3c "Motorola RAZR V3c") ([CDMA](/wiki/Code_division_multiple_access "Code division multiple access")), [Razr V3i](/wiki/Motorola_RAZR_V3i "Motorola RAZR V3i") (with upgraded camera and appearance and iTunes syncing for 100 songs), V3x (supports [3G](/wiki/3G "3G") technology and has a 2-megapixel camera), [Razr V3xx](/wiki/Motorola_RAZR_V3c#V3xx "Motorola RAZR V3c") (supports [3.5G](/wiki/3.5G "3.5G") technology) and [Razr maxx V6](/wiki/Motorola_Razr#maxx_V6 "Motorola Razr") (supports [3.5G](/wiki/3.5G "3.5G") technology and has a 2-megapixel camera) announced in July 2006.

The Razr series was marketed until July 2007, when the succeeding [Motorola Razr2](/wiki/Motorola_Razr2 "Motorola Razr2") series was released. Marketed as a more sleek and more stable design of the Razr, the Razr2 included more features, improved telephone audio quality, and a touch-sensitive external screen. The new models were the V8, the V9, and the V9m.[[64]](#cite_note-64) However, Razr2 sales were only half of the original in the same period.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

Because Motorola relied so long upon the Razr and its derivatives[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-reardon-67) and was slow to develop new products in the growing market for feature-rich touchscreen and 3G phones,[[68]](#cite_note-68) the Razr appeal declined while rival offerings like the [LG Chocolate](/wiki/LG_Chocolate "LG Chocolate"), [BlackBerry](/wiki/BlackBerry "BlackBerry"), and [iPhone](/wiki/IPhone "IPhone") captured, leading Motorola to eventually drop behind [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung_Electronics "Samsung Electronics") and [LG](/wiki/LG "LG") in market share for mobile phones.[[69]](#cite_note-bloomberg2008.03.26-69) Motorola's strategy of grabbing market share by selling tens of millions of low-cost Razrs cut into margins and resulted in heavy losses in the cellular division.[[67]](#cite_note-reardon-67)[[70]](#cite_note-bartash-70)

Motorola capitalized on the Razr too long and it was also slow adopting 3G. While [Nokia](/wiki/Microsoft_Mobile "Microsoft Mobile") managed to retain its lead of the worldwide cellular market, Motorola was surpassed first by [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung") and then [LG Electronics](/wiki/LG_Electronics "LG Electronics").[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-businessweek-72) By 2007, without new cellphones that carriers wanted to offer, Motorola sold tens of millions of Razrs and their offshoots by slashing prices, causing margins to collapse in the process.[[73]](#cite_note-73) CEO of Motorola [Ed Zander](/wiki/Ed_Zander "Ed Zander") departed for [Dell](/wiki/Dell "Dell"), while his successor failed to turn around the struggling mobile handset division.[[72]](#cite_note-businessweek-72)

Motorola continued to experience severe problems with its cellphone/handset division in the latter-2000s, recording a record $1.2 billion loss in Q4 2007.[[74]](#cite_note-74) Its global competitiveness continued to decline: from 18.4% market share in 2007, to 9.7% by 2008. By 2010 Motorola's global market share had dropped to seventh place, leading to speculation of bankruptcy of the company.[[75]](#cite_note-75) While Motorola's other businesses were thriving, the poor results from the Mobile Devices Unit as well as the 2008 financial crisis delayed the company plans to spin off the mobile division.[[76]](#cite_note-reardon2-76)

The famous “Hello Moto” ringtone, was used since at least Motorola Razr V3 series.

### Early Android smartphones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Early Android smartphones")]

#### Droid

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Droid")]

In 2008, [Sanjay Jha](/wiki/Sanjay_Jha_(businessman) "Sanjay Jha (businessman)") took over as co-chief executive officer of Motorola's mobile device division; under Jha's control, significant changes were made to Motorola's mobile phone business, including most prominently, a shift to the recently introduced [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") operating system as its sole [smartphone](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") platform, replacing both [Symbian](/wiki/Symbian "Symbian") and [Windows Mobile](/wiki/Windows_Mobile "Windows Mobile"). In August 2009, Motorola introduced the [Cliq](/wiki/Motorola_Cliq "Motorola Cliq"), its first Android device, for [T-Mobile USA](/wiki/T-Mobile_USA "T-Mobile USA"). The device also featured a user interface known as [Motoblur](/wiki/Motoblur "Motoblur"), which aimed to aggregate information from various sources, such as e-mail and [social networking services](/wiki/Social_networking_service "Social networking service"), into a consistent interface.[[77]](#cite_note-cnet-cliq-77)[[78]](#cite_note-mui-78)

A month later, Motorola unveiled [the Droid](/wiki/Motorola_Droid "Motorola Droid"), [Verizon Wireless](/wiki/Verizon_Wireless "Verizon Wireless")'s first Android phone, which was released on November 8, 2009. Backed with a marketing campaign by Verizon, which promoted the device as a direct competitor to the [iPhone](/wiki/IPhone "IPhone") with the slogan "iDon't, Droid Does", the Droid was a significant success for Motorola and Verizon. [Flurry](/wiki/Flurry_(company) "Flurry (company)") estimated that at least 250,000 Droid smartphones had been sold in its first week of availability. *[PC World](/wiki/PC_World "PC World")* considered the sales figures to be an indicator of mainstream growth for the Android platform as a whole.[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-cnet-droidunveil-80)[[81]](#cite_note-techhive-androidexplosion-81) The Droid was also named "Gadget of the Year" for 2009 by *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")*.[[82]](#cite_note-82) Other *Droid*-branded devices would be released by Verizon, although not all of them were manufactured by Motorola.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

In 2010, Motorola released the [Droid X](/wiki/Droid_X "Droid X") along with other devices such as the [Charm](/wiki/Motorola_Charm "Motorola Charm"), [Flipout](/wiki/Motorola_Flipout "Motorola Flipout"), and [i1](/wiki/Motorola_i1 "Motorola i1"). In July 2010, Motorola reported that it had sold 2.7 million smartphones during the second quarter of 2010, an increase of 400,000 units over the first quarter. Jha stated that the company was in "a strong position to continue improving our share in the rapidly growing smartphone market and improve our operating performance."[[84]](#cite_note-geekcom-q2sales-84) In its third quarter earnings report, Jha reaffirmed that the Droid X was selling "extremely well."[[85]](#cite_note-85)

#### Atrix 4G, Droid Bionic, XOOM, and Droid RAZR

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Atrix 4G, Droid Bionic, XOOM, and Droid RAZR")]

On January 5, 2011, Motorola Mobility announced that the Atrix 4G and the Droid Bionic were headed to AT&T and Verizon, respectively, with expected release dates in Q1 of 2011. The [Atrix](/wiki/Motorola_Atrix_4G "Motorola Atrix 4G") was released on February 22 as the world's first phone with both a Dual-Core Processor and 1 GB of RAM.[[86]](#cite_note-86) The phone also had optional peripherals such as a Multimedia Dock and a Laptop Dock which launched a Webtop UI.[[87]](#cite_note-87)[*[not specific enough to verify](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] On February 24, two days after the release of Atrix, the company released [Motorola Xoom](/wiki/Motorola_Xoom "Motorola Xoom"), the world's first Android 3.0 tablet,[[88]](#cite_note-88) and followed it up shortly afterwards with an update to make it the world's first Android 3.1 tablet.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

In the fourth quarter of 2011, Motorola Mobility unveiled the [Droid RAZR](/wiki/Droid_RAZR "Droid RAZR"), the world's thinnest 4G LTE smartphone at that time at just 7.1 mm. The Droid Razr featured [Kevlar](/wiki/Kevlar "Kevlar") backing, the same used in [bulletproof vests](/wiki/Bulletproof_vest "Bulletproof vest"), and a [Gorilla Glass](/wiki/Gorilla_Glass "Gorilla Glass") faceplate. The phone was very successful through Verizon Wireless, and many color variants were released. In addition, a Maxx version of the Droid RAZR with an extended battery was released at CES 2012. The Droid RAZR MAXX won CTIA's "Best Smartphone" award.[[90]](#cite_note-90) The company also announced new products by late 2011 and early 2012 such as the Xoom 2 tablets, the motoACTV fitness watch with Android, and the Droid 4 with 4G LTE for Verizon Wireless.

Though Jha managed to restore some of the lost luster to Motorola Mobility, it struggled against Samsung and [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.").[[78]](#cite_note-mui-78) Even among Android manufacturers, Motorola Mobility had dropped behind Samsung, HTC, and LG in market share by the second quarter of 2011. This may have been due to the delay in releasing 4G LTE-capable devices, as well as setting the prices of its new products too high.[[28]](#cite_note-cnet081511-28) Jha was replaced by Dennis Woodside as CEO by May 2012, when the Google acquisition was complete.

Motorola Mobility released the [Droid RAZR HD](/wiki/Droid_RAZR_HD "Droid RAZR HD") (and Droid RAZR MAXX HD) as its 2012 flagship devices, featuring improvements over 2011's RAZR. A lower end RAZR M was released, along with an [Intel](/wiki/Intel "Intel") powered RAZR i. Through late 2012 until 2013's third quarter, no further devices were released, except for the lower end RAZR D1 and D3 devices for Latin America.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Google era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Google era")]

#### Moto X (2013–2015)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Moto X (2013–2015)")]

Main article: [Moto X](/wiki/Moto_X "Moto X")

In an August 2013 interview, Motorola Mobility's vice president of product management [Lior Ron](/wiki/Lior_Ron_(business_executive) "Lior Ron (business executive)") explained that the company will focus on the production of fewer products to focus on quality rather than quantity. Ron stated, "Our mandate from Google, from Larry, is really to innovate and take long-term bets. When you have that sort of mentality, it's about quality and not quantity".[[91]](#cite_note-91)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Moto_X_%2811236439494%29.jpg/250px-Moto_X_%2811236439494%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Moto_X_(11236439494).jpg)

1st generation Moto X

Speaking at the D11 conference in [Palos Verdes](/wiki/Palos_Verdes "Palos Verdes"), California, in May 2013, Motorola Mobility CEO [Dennis Woodside](/wiki/Dennis_Woodside "Dennis Woodside") announced that a new mobile device would be built by his company at a 500,000 square-feet facility near [Fort Worth](/wiki/Fort_Worth,_Texas "Fort Worth, Texas"), Texas, formerly used by Nokia. The facility will employ 2,000 people by August 2013 and the new phone, to be named "[Moto X](/wiki/Moto_X_(1st_generation) "Moto X (1st generation)")", will be available to the public in October 2013.[[92]](#cite_note-92) The Moto X featured [Google Now](/wiki/Google_Now "Google Now") software, and an array of sensors and two microprocessors that will mean that users can “interact with [the phone] in very different ways than you can with other devices”. Media reports suggested that the phone will be able to activate functions preemptively based on an "awareness" of what the user is doing at any given moment.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

On July 3, 2013, Motorola Mobility released a full-page color advertisement in many prominent newspapers across the US. It claimed that Motorola Mobility's next flagship phone will be "the first smartphone designed, engineered, and assembled in the United States".[[94]](#cite_note-94) On the same day that the advertisement was published, *[ABC News](/wiki/ABC_News_(United_States) "ABC News (United States)")* reported that customers will be able to choose the color of the phone, as well as add custom engravings and wallpaper at the time of purchase.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

In early July 2013, the *[Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")* reported that Motorola Mobility would spend nearly US$500 million on global advertising and marketing for the device. The amount is equivalent to half of Apple's total advertising budget for 2012.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

On August 1, 2013, Motorola Mobility unveiled the [first-generation Moto X](/wiki/Moto_X_(1st_generation) "Moto X (1st generation)") smartphone. It was released on August 23, 2013, in the US and Canada.[[97]](#cite_note-97)

On September 5, 2014, Motorola Mobility released the [second-generation Moto X](/wiki/Moto_X_(2nd_generation) "Moto X (2nd generation)"). This continued the trend of the company letting consumers customize their devices through their Moto Maker website, and added new customization options like additional real wood choices and new leather options. The device also got many increases in specs. With a new 5.2-inch (13 cm) [1080p](/wiki/1080p "1080p") [super AMOLED](/wiki/Super_AMOLED "Super AMOLED") [pentile](/wiki/Pentile "Pentile") display, a faster 2.5 GHz [Qualcomm](/wiki/Qualcomm "Qualcomm") [Snapdragon](/wiki/Qualcomm_Snapdragon "Qualcomm Snapdragon") 801 processor, and an improved 13-megapixel rear camera capable of recording [4k resolution](/wiki/4k_resolution "4k resolution") video with a dual [LED flash](/wiki/LED_flash "LED flash"). The device also came with new software features along with new [infrared](/wiki/Infrared "Infrared") proximity sensors.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Motorola_Moto_X_Style_%2822033955888%29.jpg/250px-Motorola_Moto_X_Style_%2822033955888%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Motorola_Moto_X_Style_(22033955888).jpg)

Moto X Style

The [Moto X Play](/wiki/Moto_X_Play "Moto X Play") and [Moto X Style](/wiki/Moto_X_Style "Moto X Style") smartphones were announced in July 2015, and were released in September 2015.[[48]](#cite_note-VergeMoto-48) Many customers who have ordered customized Moto X Pure Editions via Motorola Mobility's website have experienced delays receiving their devices. These delays have been attributed to issues including manufacturing issues, lack of parts needed to complete assembly of custom phones (black fronts, Verizon SIM cards and 64 GB versions), a possible redesign due to initial phones having a defect that causes one of the front facing speakers to rattle at high volume and multiple day delays clearing US customs at FedEx's Memphis, Tennessee hub due to issues related to the import paperwork.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Moto X Force](/wiki/Moto_X_Force "Moto X Force") was launched on October 27, 2015. In the US, it was branded as the Droid Turbo 2 and was Motorola Mobility's flagship device of the year, offering Snapdragon 810 processor and 3 GB of memory. Like other Moto X devices, it was customizable through Moto Maker. This was Motorola Mobility's first smartphone that featured the company's "ShatterShield" technology, which consists of two touchscreen elements, reinforced by an internal aluminum frame to make it resistant to bending and cracking, although this does not protect against scratches or other superficial screen damage. The top layer of the display is designed to be user-replaceable. The screen and case also have a water repellent nanocoating to protect the device from liquids that could damage internal components.

The [Moto X4](/wiki/Moto_X4 "Moto X4") was introduced in August 2017. It featured a 5.2-inch 1080p IPS display compared to Moto X 2014, and 3, 4, or 6 GB of RAM depending on versions. There were three iterations: a retail one from Motorola Mobility, an [Android One](/wiki/Android_One "Android One") version from Google, and an [Amazon Prime](/wiki/Amazon_Prime "Amazon Prime") edition. Unlike its predecessors, the Moto X4 did not offer some older Moto X exclusive features such as the Moto Maker. The phone came with [Android Nougat](/wiki/Android_Nougat "Android Nougat") 7.1.1 and Moto features, with support of 'A/B Partitions' and 'seamless update'. It was updated to [Android Oreo](/wiki/Android_Oreo "Android Oreo")[[98]](#cite_note-98) in late 2017 and [Android Pie](/wiki/Android_Pie "Android Pie")[[99]](#cite_note-99) in 2018. It was succeeded by the Motorola One lineup since 2018.

#### Droid Mini, Ultra and Maxx

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Droid Mini, Ultra and Maxx")]

Main articles: [Droid Mini](/wiki/Droid_Mini "Droid Mini"), [Droid Ultra](/wiki/Droid_Ultra "Droid Ultra"), and [Droid Maxx](/wiki/Droid_Maxx "Droid Maxx")

Droid Mini, Droid Ultra and Droid Maxx were announced in a Verizon press conference on July 23, 2013. These phones share similar design with the predecessing Droid Razr HD lines in different screen and battery sizes, while all featuring the same [Motorola X8 Mobile Computing System](/wiki/Motorola_X8_Mobile_Computing_System "Motorola X8 Mobile Computing System") as the first-generation Moto X, with exclusive features like Motorola Active Notifications and 'OK Google' on device neural-based voice recognition system.

In September 2015, Droid Maxx 2 were launched as Verizon exclusives in the US market, which share the same overall design as the [Moto X Style](/wiki/Moto_X_Style "Moto X Style"), with Verizon software on board. Unlike its Moto X counterpart, Droid Maxx 2 did not support Moto Maker for customization.

#### Moto G

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Moto G")]

Main article: [Moto G](/wiki/Moto_G "Moto G")

On November 13, 2013, Motorola Mobility unveiled the [first generation Moto G](/wiki/Moto_G_(1st_generation) "Moto G (1st generation)"), a relatively low-cost smartphone. The Moto G had been launched in several markets, including the UK, United States, France, Germany, India and parts of Latin America and Asia. The Moto G was available in the United States, unlocked, for a starting price of US$179. The device is geared toward global markets and some US models support [4G LTE](/wiki/LTE_(telecommunication) "LTE (telecommunication)"). Unlike the Moto X, the Moto G was not manufactured in the United States.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Motorola_Moto_G2_7137.jpg/250px-Motorola_Moto_G2_7137.jpg)](/wiki/File:Motorola_Moto_G2_7137.jpg)

2nd generation Moto G

On September 5, 2014, Motorola Mobility released its successor to the 2013 version of the Moto G, called the [Moto G (2nd generation)](/wiki/Moto_G_(2nd_generation) "Moto G (2nd generation)"). It came with a larger screen, higher resolution camera, along with dual front-facing stereo speakers.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

On July 28, 2015, Motorola Mobility released the third generation of the Moto G series, called the [Moto G (3rd generation)](/wiki/Moto_G_(3rd_generation) "Moto G (3rd generation)"), in a worldwide press conference in New Delhi, India. It retained the same screen as before but upgraded the processor and RAM. Furthermore, it has an IPx7 water-resistance certification and comes into two variants – one with 1 GB of memory and 8 GB of storage, and the other with 2 GB of memory and 16 GB of storage. The device launched with Android version 5.1.1.[[48]](#cite_note-VergeMoto-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Moto_G4_Plus_Camera.jpg/250px-Moto_G4_Plus_Camera.jpg)](/wiki/File:Moto_G4_Plus_Camera.jpg)

Moto G4 Plus

In May 2016, Motorola Mobility released three fourth generation Moto G smartphones: [Moto G⁴](/wiki/Moto_G4 "Moto G4"), Moto G⁴ Plus, and Moto G⁴ Play.

On February 26, 2017, Motorola Mobility released two fifth generation Moto G smartphones during [Mobile World Congress](/wiki/Mobile_World_Congress "Mobile World Congress"): [Moto G5](/wiki/Moto_G5 "Moto G5") and Moto G5 Plus.[[102]](#cite_note-102) The company added two 'special edition' models to the Moto G lineup, the [Moto G5S and Moto G5S Plus](/wiki/Moto_G5S#Moto_G5S_and_G5S_Plus "Moto G5S"), on August 1.[[103]](#cite_note-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104)

In May 2018, Motorola Mobility released the sixth generation of the Moto G lineup in three variants, the [G6, G6 Plus and the G6 Play](/wiki/Moto_G6 "Moto G6"). The G6 and G6 Plus have two rear cameras, and a larger screen with an aspect ratio of 18:9. The G6 Plus is capable of taking 4K video. The specification ranges from 3/32 GB to 6/128 GB.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Moto_G7_Plus_XT1965-2_LATAM.jpg/250px-Moto_G7_Plus_XT1965-2_LATAM.jpg)](/wiki/File:Moto_G7_Plus_XT1965-2_LATAM.jpg)

Moto G7 Plus

In February 2019, Motorola Mobility launched the Moto G7 with 3 extra variants, the Moto G7 Play, Moto G7 Power and the Moto G7 Plus.[[105]](#cite_note-105)

#### Moto E

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Moto E")]

Main article: [Motorola Moto E](/wiki/Motorola_Moto_E "Motorola Moto E")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Moto_E5_Plus_XT1924-1_Black.png/250px-Moto_E5_Plus_XT1924-1_Black.png)](/wiki/File:Moto_E5_Plus_XT1924-1_Black.png)

Moto E5 Plus

The [Moto E (1st generation)](/wiki/Moto_E_(1st_generation) "Moto E (1st generation)") was announced and launched on May 13, 2014. It was an entry-level device intended to compete against feature phones by providing a durable, low-cost device for first-time smartphone owners or budget-minded consumers, with a particular emphasis on emerging markets. The Moto E shipped with a stock version of [Android 4.4 "KitKat"](/wiki/Android_KitKat "Android KitKat").

The [Moto E (2nd generation)](/wiki/Moto_E_(2nd_generation) "Moto E (2nd generation)") was announced and launched on March 10, 2015, in India. Released in the wake of its successful first generation, the second generation of the Moto E series still aims to provide a smooth experience to budget-oriented consumers. It increased the screen size to 4.5 inches but kept the resolution at 540 × 960px. It came in two versions, a 3G-only one powered by a Snapdragon 200 chipset and a 4G LTE version powered by a Snapdragon 410 chipset. As before, it shipped with a stock version of [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") [5.0 "Lollipop"](/wiki/Android_Lollipop "Android Lollipop").

In 2015 Motorola Mobility marketed the 2nd generation Moto E with the promise of continual updates and support, "And while other smartphones in this category don't always support upgrades, we won't forget about you, and we'll make sure your Moto E stays up to date after you buy it." However, 219 days after launch Motorola announced that the device would not receive an upgrade from Lollipop to [6.0 "Marshmallow"](/wiki/Android_Marshmallow "Android Marshmallow").[[106]](#cite_note-106) It was later announced that the LTE variant of the device would receive an upgrade to Marshmallow in Canada, Europe, Latin America, and Asia (excluding China). China and the US carrier-branded versions of the device remained on Lollipop,[[107]](#cite_note-107) with a minor upgrade to version 5.1.[[108]](#cite_note-108) However, the 2nd generation Moto E in the USA did continue to receive support via Android Security Patch updates until at least the October 1, 2016 patch for the LTE variant[[109]](#cite_note-109) and the November 1, 2016 patch for the non-LTE variant.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

#### Google Nexus 6 / Moto X Pro

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Google Nexus 6 / Moto X Pro")]

Main article: [Nexus 6](/wiki/Nexus_6 "Nexus 6")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Nexus_6.png/250px-Nexus_6.png)](/wiki/File:Nexus_6.png)

Nexus 6

The Nexus 6 was announced October 15, 2014 by Motorola Mobility in partnership with [Google](/wiki/Google_Inc "Google Inc"). It was the first 6-inch smartphone in the mainstream market, and came with many high-end specs. It was the successor to the Nexus 5, Google's previous flagship phone from their [Google Nexus](/wiki/Google_Nexus "Google Nexus") series of devices. Its design was similar to the [Moto X (2nd generation)](/wiki/Moto_X_(2nd_generation) "Moto X (2nd generation)") but with a larger display and dual, front-facing speakers rather than the single front-facing speaker on the Moto X. It was the first phone running vanilla [Android Lollipop](/wiki/Android_Lollipop "Android Lollipop"), receiving software updates directly from [Google](/wiki/Google "Google"). It was later updated to [Android Marshmallow](/wiki/Android_Marshmallow "Android Marshmallow") in 2015 and [Android Nougat](/wiki/Android_Nougat "Android Nougat") in 2016, though later versions of Android 7.X took some time to arrive, and it never received Android 7.1.2 update, ending its support with Android 7.1.1 in the end of 2017.

On January 26, 2015, Motorola Mobility announced that they would sell the [Moto X Pro](/wiki/Moto_X_Pro "Moto X Pro") in China. The [Moto X Pro](/wiki/Moto_X_Pro "Moto X Pro") was similar to the Nexus 6 in terms of hardware, but excluded all of Google's services and applications. The phone was released in April 2015 with Android 5.0.2 'Lollipop'. However, it never received any Android version update throughout its lifetime despite the same hardware as the Nexus 6.

#### Droid Turbo / Moto Maxx / Moto Turbo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Droid Turbo / Moto Maxx / Moto Turbo")]

Main article: [Droid Turbo](/wiki/Droid_Turbo "Droid Turbo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Motorola_Droid_Turbo.jpg/250px-Motorola_Droid_Turbo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Motorola_Droid_Turbo.jpg)

Droid Turbo

The Droid Turbo (Moto Maxx in South America and Mexico, Moto Turbo in India) features a 3900 mAh battery lasting up to two days. Motorola claims an additional eight hours of use after only fifteen minutes of charging with the included Turbo Charger. The device is finished in ballistic nylon over a Kevlar fiber layer and is protected by a water repellent nano-coating.[[111]](#cite_note-Motorola_Blog_-_Moto_Maxx-111) Droid Turbo uses a quad-core Snapdragon 805 processor clocked at 2.7 GHz, 3 GB RAM, a 21-megapixel camera with 4K video, 5.2-inch screen with resolution of 2560 × 1440 pixels. The Droid Turbo includes 32 or 64 GB of internal storage, while the Moto Maxx is only available in 64 GB.[[112]](#cite_note-Android_Authority_-_Moto_Maxx-112)

In late 2015, Droid Turbo 2 was introduced as a Verizon exclusive, which was a rebrand of [Moto X Force](/wiki/Moto_X_Force "Moto X Force"). It was the first Droid device to offer Moto Mods for extensive customization, and ShatterShield display technologies.

#### Moto Z and Moto Mods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Moto Z and Moto Mods")]

Main articles: [Moto Z](/wiki/Moto_Z "Moto Z") and [Moto Mods](/wiki/Moto_Mods "Moto Mods")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Moto_Z_Hasselblad_True_Zoom_%2832437766740%29.jpg/250px-Moto_Z_Hasselblad_True_Zoom_%2832437766740%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Moto_Z_Hasselblad_True_Zoom_(32437766740).jpg)

Moto Z 1st generation with Moto Style Shell

The Moto Z lineup was introduced in June 2016. The smartphone features Motorola Mobility's Moto Mods platform, in which the user can magnetically attach accessories or "Mods" to the back of the phone, including a projector, style shells, a Hasselblad-branded camera lens, and a JBL speaker. There were three versions of the original Moto Z. The global flagship model Moto Z, and Moto Z Droid as a Verizon exclusive, were introduced as the thinnest premium smartphone in the world, according to Motorola, and featured a 13-megapixel camera with 4K video, 5.5-inch screen and 4 GB of RAM. and an underclocked Snapdragon 820 chipset at 1.8 GHz, but was able to unlock with root permission granted.[[113]](#cite_note-Motorola_Blog_-_Moto_Z-113) Moto Z Play, on the other hand, featured a less powerful processor and a bigger battery. Moto Z Force Droid, only introduced as a Verizon exclusive, featured the Snapdragon 820 chipset with standard frequency, a display with Motorola ShatterShield technology and a 21-megapixel camera. These phones ship with near stock [Android 6.0](/wiki/Android_6.0 "Android 6.0") 'Marshmallow' with usual Moto experiences. They were later updated to [Android Nougat](/wiki/Android_Nougat "Android Nougat") and [Android Oreo](/wiki/Android_Oreo "Android Oreo") in early 2017 and 2018, a substantial delay compared to older Moto X models.

[Moto Z2 Play](/wiki/Moto_Z2_Play "Moto Z2 Play") was launched in June 2017 with updated Moto experience, a slightly faster processor than [Moto Z Play](/wiki/Moto_Z_Play "Moto Z Play"), and [Android Nougat](/wiki/Android_Nougat "Android Nougat"). Moto Z2 Force was launched in July 2017, and was the last Motorola flagship phone with up-to-date processor as for now. It was the first non-Google phone to feature 'A/B partition' and 'seamless update' features of [Android Nougat](/wiki/Android_Nougat "Android Nougat"), allowing users to install update in the background and finish with just a restart.[[114]](#cite_note-XDA_Developers_-_Motorola's_Moto_Z2_Force_uses_Google's_A/B_Partitioning_for_Seamless_Updates-114) It was updated to [Android Oreo](/wiki/Android_Oreo "Android Oreo") in the end of 2017, which was fairly speedy compared to other Android OEMs. In China, the phones run [ZUI](/wiki/ZUI "ZUI") from ZUK, a subsidiary of Lenovo instead of near stock Android,[[115]](#cite_note-115) and Moto Z2 Force was rebranded as 'Moto Z 2018'.[[116]](#cite_note-116) There were also several [Moto Mods](/wiki/Moto_Mods "Moto Mods") released in 2017, including a Motorola TurboPower Moto Mod with fast charging capabilities.

The [Moto Z3](/wiki/Moto_Z3 "Moto Z3") lineup was released in August 2018, consisting of Moto Z3 Play with Snapdragon 6-series SoC and Moto Z3 with last generation 8-series SoC, both supporting all Moto Mods introduced.[[117]](#cite_note-117) The 5G Moto Mod was introduced alongside the Moto Z3 lineup, and was a Verizon exclusive at launch in early 2019. It enables 5G connectivity for Moto Z2 Force and later Moto Z devices[[118]](#cite_note-118) when the mod is attached on the phone. The phones launched with [Android Oreo](/wiki/Android_Oreo "Android Oreo") and was later updated to [Android Pie](/wiki/Android_Pie "Android Pie") with a similar software experience as earlier Moto Z devices.

The [Moto Z4](/wiki/Moto_Z4 "Moto Z4") was launched in May 2019 with a 48-megapixel camera sensor and enhanced night sight features, as well as an intergraded fingerprint sensor. It featured a Snapdragon 675 SoC, 4 GB of memory, and launched with [Android Pie](/wiki/Android_Pie "Android Pie"). Unlike older Moto Z models, this phone was focused on upper mid-range market, and came with a Moto 360 Camera Mod in the box.

#### Moto M

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Moto M")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Motorola_Moto_M.png/250px-Motorola_Moto_M.png)](/wiki/File:Motorola_Moto_M.png)

Moto M

The Moto M was introduced in late 2016. It was a mid-range device launched in markets such as mainland China and Hong Kong, [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"), and [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"). It featured a [MediaTek](/wiki/MediaTek "MediaTek") Helio P10 SoC and 3 or 4 GB of memory depending on storage, and ran near-stock Android.[[119]](#cite_note-119) Despite the Moto branding, the bootloader and software update software came from Lenovo directly instead of Motorola Mobility, similar to the Moto E3 Power.

#### Moto C

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Moto C")]

Main article: [Moto C](/wiki/Moto_C "Moto C")

The [Moto C](/wiki/Moto_C "Moto C") was announced in 2017 as a low-end device, slotting in below the Moto E as the cheapest in the Moto range. The base model includes a 5-inch display, [MediaTek](/wiki/MediaTek "MediaTek") MT6737M SoC, 1 GB of memory, a 5-megapixel rear-facing camera, and an LED flash for the front-facing camera. It is accompanied by a higher-end model, the Moto C Plus, which features a more powerful processor, larger battery, 8-megapixel camera, and a 720p display.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

#### Motorola One lineup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Motorola One lineup")]

Main article: [Motorola One](/wiki/Motorola_One "Motorola One")

In 2018, Motorola Mobility launched the Motorola One lineup as upper mid-range replacements for the [Moto X4](/wiki/Moto_X4 "Moto X4"). In August 2018, the first phones in the lineup, the Motorola One and Motorola One Power were launched. Both phones featured dual camera setup, displays with 'notches' for sensors and front-facing camera, and they were all in the [Android One](/wiki/Android_One "Android One") program by Google, with guaranteed three-year security updates. The phones launched with [Android Oreo](/wiki/Android_Oreo "Android Oreo") 8.1, and were later updated to [Android Pie](/wiki/Android_Pie "Android Pie") and [Android 10](/wiki/Android_10 "Android 10") at the end of 2018 and 2019, respectively.

Motorola One Vision and Motorola One Action were introduced in May and August 2019. These devices all feature [Samsung Exynos](/wiki/Samsung_Exynos "Samsung Exynos") processors. Moto One Vision featured dual camera setup, with the main shooter being 48-megapixel. It featured a 'hole punch' display for front camera, and 27 W TurboPower fast charging. The Motorola One Action featured a triple camera setup with an ultra-wide lens. Both phones were a part of [Android One](/wiki/Android_One "Android One") program by Google. They launched with [Android Pie](/wiki/Android_Pie "Android Pie"), and were updated to [Android 10](/wiki/Android_10 "Android 10") in early January 2020.

#### Motorola Razr (2020)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Motorola Razr (2020)")]

Main article: [Motorola Razr (2020)](/wiki/Motorola_Razr_(2020) "Motorola Razr (2020)")

The Razr is the first foldable smartphone from Motorola Mobility, utilizing a similar design as the original Razr. It was introduced in November 2019 and was expected to launch in 2020. It featured mid-range Snapdragon 7-series SoC and [Android Pie](/wiki/Android_Pie "Android Pie"), with promised updates to [Android 10](/wiki/Android_10 "Android 10") in 2020.

#### Motorola Edge & Edge+

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Motorola Edge & Edge+")]

Main articles: [Motorola Edge](/wiki/Motorola_Edge "Motorola Edge") and [Motorola Edge+](/wiki/Motorola_Edge%2B "Motorola Edge+")

The Motorola Edge and Motorola Edge+ were introduced in April 2020. The Edge uses the [Snapdragon 765G](/wiki/List_of_Qualcomm_Snapdragon_processors#Snapdragon_750G_and_765/765G/768G_5G_(2020) "List of Qualcomm Snapdragon processors"), while the Edge+ uses the [Snapdragon 865](/wiki/Snapdragon_865 "Snapdragon 865"); both feature Android 10.0, standard 5G and a curved 90 Hz OLED display. The Edge+ was the first Motorola phone to use a 108-megapixel sensor for the main camera with 6K video recording, and marked a return to flagship devices for Motorola Mobility.

The ThinkPhone is an Android-based smartphone marketed toward enterprise users. It was announced in January 2023 and is still in production. The device includes Lenovo’s [ThinkShield](/w/index.php?title=ThinkShield&action=edit&redlink=1 "ThinkShield (page does not exist)") security platform, which provides hardware and software-level security features. The ThinkPhone supports integration with Lenovo’s [ThinkPad](/wiki/ThinkPad "ThinkPad") and [ThinkCentre](/wiki/ThinkCentre "ThinkCentre") products, enabling cross-device features such as shared clipboard and file transfer. It is compatible with Android Enterprise management tools (MDM's), including zero-touch enrollment and remote configuration. It runs on Android 14 (ThinkPhone 25), 8 GB LDDR4 RAM, MediaTek Dimensity 7300 processor, and 256 GB of storage. The device is primarily available in select regions, including North America and parts of Europe and Asia. It competes with [Samsung's Knox](/wiki/Samsung_Knox "Samsung Knox") platform with each platform operating in somewhat different capacities.

## Brand licensing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Brand licensing")]

The company has licensed its brand through the years to several companies and a variety of home products and mobile phone accessories have been released. Motorola Mobility created a dedicated "Motorola Home" website for these products,[[123]](#cite_note-Motorola_Home-123) which sells corded and cordless phones, cable modems and routers, baby monitors, home monitoring systems and pet safety systems. In 2015, Motorola Mobility sold its brand rights for accessories to [Binatone](/wiki/Binatone "Binatone"), which already was the official licensee for certain home products. This deal includes brand rights for all mobile and car accessories under the Motorola brand.[[124]](#cite_note-Motorola_-_Binatone_deal-124)

In 2016, [Zoom Telephonics](/wiki/Zoom_Telephonics "Zoom Telephonics") was granted the worldwide brand rights for home networking products, including cable modems, routers, Wi-Fi range extenders and related networking products.[[125]](#cite_note-Zoom(R)_Telephonics_Expands_Motorola_Brand_License-125)

In March 2026, Motorola Mobility launched a partnership with [GrapheneOS](/wiki/GrapheneOS "GrapheneOS") to support the hardened OS on their phones.[[126]](#cite_note-126) This ends the previously exclusive support for GrapheneOS by [Google Pixel](/wiki/Google_Pixel "Google Pixel") devices. There is no word yet what the first GrapheneOS supported Motorola phones will be.[[127]](#cite_note-127)

## Sponsorship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Motorola_Mobility&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Sponsorship")]

Since 2021 Motorola is the main sponsor of the [Milwaukee Bucks](/wiki/Milwaukee_Bucks "Milwaukee Bucks") of the NBA
From 2022, Motorola was the main kit sponsor of Italian football club [AC Monza](/wiki/AC_Monza "AC Monza").

[The Athletic](/wiki/The_Athletic "The Athletic") reported on August 1st, 2024, Motorola signed a multiyear long deal with the [Chicago Cubs](/wiki/Chicago_Cubs "Chicago Cubs") to have their logo placed on a uniform patch. The deal also made Motorola the "official smartphone" brand for the team.[[128]](#cite_note-128)

In September 2024, Motorola became the global smartphone partner of [Formula 1](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") from 2025 onwards,[[129]](#cite_note-129) and in the following month Motorola also has inked the same deal with [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA").[[130]](#cite_note-130) Both were made as a part of Lenovo's partnership with Formula 1 and FIFA.
